enrichment.short_description: OpenStack is a free, open-source cloud computing platform that lets organizations pool compute, storage, and networking resources to build and operate public or private Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) clouds.
enrichment.company_size: Nonprofit foundation (OpenInfra Foundation, part of the Linux Foundation) with thousands of global contributors
enrichment.seo_title: OpenStack Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenStack in 2026: what the open-source cloud platform does, how its services fit together, real deployment costs, and how it compares to AWS and VMware.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenStack is a free, open-source cloud computing platform used to build and operate Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) clouds, both public and private. It launched in July 2010 as a joint project between Rackspace Hosting and NASA, combining Rackspace's Cloud Files object storage platform with NASA's Nebula compute platform; the first release, codenamed 'Austin,' shipped in October 2010. Governance moved to the independent, nonprofit OpenStack Foundation in September 2012, which was renamed the Open Infrastructure Foundation (OpenInfra Foundation) around 2020-2021 to reflect a broader portfolio of adjacent projects including Kata Containers, Zuul, Airship, and StarlingX. In March 2025 the OpenInfra Foundation joined the Linux Foundation as the OpenInfra division, placing OpenStack under the same nonprofit umbrella as Kubernetes. Written primarily in Python and licensed under Apache 2.0, OpenStack is organized as a set of interoperable services rather than a single application: Nova handles compute/VM provisioning, Neutron handles software-defined networking, Cinder and Swift provide block and object storage, Keystone provides identity and access management, Glance manages images, Ironic provisions bare metal, Heat handles orchestration, and Horizon is the web dashboard. Because it is fully open source, there is no license fee to run it; organizations either self-host on their own hardware or buy supported distributions and managed OpenStack services from vendors such as Red Hat, Canonical, Mirantis, VEXXHOST, and Rackspace, which is where most real-world spend occurs. OpenStack's core audience is telecoms building NFV and 5G infrastructure, research institutions and universities (including large deployments like CERN's), government agencies with data-sovereignty requirements, and large enterprises that want AWS- or Azure-style self-service cloud provisioning on infrastructure they control. It differs from a single hypervisor by providing a full multi-tenant cloud operating system, and it differs from Kubernetes by managing virtual machines and infrastructure rather than containers; the two are commonly deployed together, with OpenStack as the IaaS layer under a Kubernetes control plane. Its main competitors and alternatives include proprietary public clouds (AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud), VMware vSphere/vCloud for private virtualization, and other open-source options such as Apache CloudStack, Proxmox VE, and OpenNebula. As one of the largest and longest-running open-source infrastructure projects, OpenStack remains a default choice for organizations needing a vendor-neutral, self-operated cloud platform at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenStack is a modular, open-source cloud operating system that turns pools of physical compute, storage, and networking hardware into a self-service Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud. Rather than shipping as one monolithic product, it is a collection of independent but interoperable services that administrators combine based on what capabilities they need.

Originally created in 2010 by Rackspace and NASA, OpenStack is now stewarded by the OpenInfra Foundation, a nonprofit that became part of the Linux Foundation in 2025. Thousands of individual contributors and hundreds of member organizations, including major cloud vendors, telecoms, and research institutions, continue to develop the project on a roughly twice-yearly release cadence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, OpenStack provides compute orchestration through Nova, software-defined networking through Neutron, and both block and object storage through Cinder and Swift. Keystone unifies identity and authentication across every service, while Glance manages virtual machine images and Horizon offers a web-based dashboard for administrators and tenants alike.

Beyond the core, optional services extend OpenStack into bare-metal provisioning (Ironic), orchestration and auto-scaling (Heat), container-native workloads, and telecom-grade networking for NFV use cases. This a-la-carte architecture is what lets a small research lab run a minimal deployment while a telecom operator runs a massive multi-region cloud on the same underlying platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenStack itself carries no license cost; the software is free to download, deploy, and modify under the Apache 2.0 license. The real cost of running OpenStack comes from hardware, staff time, and optional commercial support.

Most organizations either self-host and self-support OpenStack with in-house engineers, or purchase a supported distribution or managed-OpenStack offering from vendors such as Red Hat, Canonical, Mirantis, or VEXXHOST, whose pricing is typically custom and quoted per deployment size and support tier.

features.title: Compute orchestration (Nova)
features.description: Provisions and manages virtual machine instances across a pool of hypervisor nodes, including scheduling, scaling, and live migration.

features.title: Software-defined networking (Neutron)
features.description: Gives tenants their own virtual networks, routers, load balancers, firewalls, and floating IPs, independent of the underlying physical network.

features.title: Block and object storage (Cinder and Swift)
features.description: Cinder provides persistent block storage volumes for instances; Swift provides highly durable, API-driven object storage similar to Amazon S3.

features.title: Identity and access management (Keystone)
features.description: Centralized authentication, multi-tenancy, and role-based access control shared across every OpenStack service.

features.title: Bare-metal provisioning (Ironic)
features.description: Provisions physical servers using the same workflow as virtual machines, useful for high-performance and GPU/AI workloads.

features.title: Orchestration and automation (Heat)
features.description: Template-driven infrastructure-as-code for defining and auto-scaling multi-resource application stacks.

features.title: Web dashboard (Horizon)
features.description: A self-service web UI for administrators and tenants to manage instances, networks, storage, and images without using the CLI or API directly.

pros: Fully open source with no licensing fees, and no vendor lock-in.
Highly modular, so deployments can be scaled from a small lab to a telecom-grade, multi-region cloud.
Backed by a mature governance model and a large, active contributor and vendor ecosystem.
Gives organizations full control over data residency and hardware, which matters for regulated industries.
Proven at massive scale in production by telecoms, research institutions, and public clouds.
cons: Steep learning curve and significant operational overhead to deploy and maintain compared to public cloud.
Requires dedicated infrastructure engineering staff; total cost of ownership can exceed public cloud for smaller workloads.
Upgrades between releases can be complex, especially for heavily customized deployments.
Feature parity and documentation quality vary across the many optional sub-projects.
Smaller ecosystem of third-party tools and integrations compared to AWS or Azure.

faqs.question: Is OpenStack free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenStack's source code is free under the Apache 2.0 license. Costs come from the hardware you run it on and any optional commercial support or managed distribution you purchase.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenStack today?
faqs.answer: OpenStack is governed by the OpenInfra Foundation, which became part of the Linux Foundation in March 2025. Development is driven by a global community of individual contributors and member companies.

faqs.question: Is OpenStack the same as Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: No. OpenStack is an IaaS platform that manages virtual machines, storage, and networking. Kubernetes orchestrates containers. The two are often deployed together, with OpenStack providing the underlying infrastructure for a Kubernetes cluster.

faqs.question: What companies use OpenStack in production?
faqs.answer: OpenStack powers infrastructure at telecoms (for NFV and 5G core networks), research organizations such as CERN, public sector agencies, and large enterprises that need a self-operated, AWS-like private cloud.

faqs.question: How does OpenStack compare to VMware?
faqs.answer: VMware is a proprietary, license-based virtualization and private cloud suite, while OpenStack is open source and free to license. OpenStack generally requires more in-house expertise but avoids VMware's licensing costs and lock-in.

faqs.question: Can I get commercial support for OpenStack?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vendors including Red Hat, Canonical, Mirantis, VEXXHOST, and Rackspace offer supported distributions and managed OpenStack services with custom pricing.

faqs.question: What programming language is OpenStack written in?
faqs.answer: OpenStack's core services are written primarily in Python, with REST APIs used for inter-service and external communication.

use_cases.title: Telecom NFV and 5G infrastructure
use_cases.description: Telecom operators use OpenStack as the virtualization layer for Network Functions Virtualization, running 5G core and edge network functions on self-operated infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Research and academic private clouds
use_cases.description: Universities and research institutions such as CERN run OpenStack to give scientists self-service compute and storage for large-scale data processing without relying on public cloud spend.

use_cases.title: Enterprise private and hybrid cloud
use_cases.description: Enterprises with data-sovereignty, compliance, or cost-control requirements deploy OpenStack to offer AWS-like self-service provisioning on their own data center hardware.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT and platform engineering teams

features: Full access to all OpenStack services and source code
Available from Red Hat, Canonical, Mirantis, VEXXHOST, and others

enrichment.short_description: OpenStreetMap (OSM) is a free, editable map of the world built and maintained by a global community of volunteer mappers and governed by the nonprofit OpenStreetMap Foundation.
enrichment.company_size: Nonprofit foundation with a small paid staff; millions of registered volunteer contributors worldwide
enrichment.seo_title: OpenStreetMap Review 2026: Features, Data Licensing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: What OpenStreetMap is, how its open map data and editing tools work, its licensing terms, and how it compares to Google Maps and Mapbox in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenStreetMap (OSM) is a free, collaboratively edited geographic database and map of the world, often described as 'Wikipedia for maps.' It was founded in 2004 by Steve Coast while he was a student at University College London, motivated by the fact that most map data at the time was proprietary and restrictively licensed even in countries like the United Kingdom. The OpenStreetMap Foundation (OSMF), a UK-registered nonprofit headquartered in Cambridge, England, was established in 2006 to own the infrastructure, hold the data license, and support the project's growth; it operates with a small paid staff and is funded by donations, corporate memberships, and sponsorships rather than by selling the map data itself. OSM's underlying data is contributed and edited by millions of registered volunteers using tools like the browser-based iD editor and the more advanced desktop JOSM editor, sourced from GPS traces, aerial and satellite imagery, and local knowledge; edits are tracked with full version history, similar to a wiki. The dataset is released under the Open Database License (ODbL), which allows free use, modification, and redistribution as long as derivative databases remain open and attribution is given. Because the raw data is free, an entire ecosystem of commercial and nonprofit services is built on top of it, including tile-hosting and geocoding providers (Mapbox, MapTiler, Geofabrik, Thunderforest), routing engines (OSRM, GraphHopper, Valhalla), and the humanitarian mapping community (Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team, or HOT), which mobilizes volunteers to rapidly map disaster and crisis zones for aid organizations. OSM's target audience spans app developers who need map tiles or geocoding without per-request licensing fees, GIS professionals and researchers who need transparent, inspectable geographic data, transit and logistics companies building routing products, and civic-tech and humanitarian organizations. Its main points of differentiation from proprietary alternatives like Google Maps or Apple Maps are that the data is free to use commercially (subject to attribution), fully inspectable and editable by anyone, and not tied to a single corporate provider's terms of service or pricing changes. Competitors and adjacent players include Google Maps Platform, HERE Technologies, TomTom, and Apple Maps on the proprietary side, and commercial OSM-data providers on the open side. Twenty years after launch, OpenStreetMap remains the largest open geographic dataset in the world and a foundational layer for a large share of the world's mapping and navigation software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenStreetMap is a free, editable database of the world's geography, built entirely by volunteer contributors and released under an open data license. It covers roads, buildings, land use, points of interest, footpaths, and countless other geographic features, all sourced from GPS traces, imagery, surveys, and local knowledge.

The project is stewarded by the OpenStreetMap Foundation, a UK nonprofit, which owns the servers and infrastructure and holds the data license on behalf of the community, but does not control or own the content itself; that belongs collectively to its contributors under the Open Database License.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Contributors edit the map using the browser-based iD editor for quick changes or the more powerful desktop JOSM editor for detailed mapping work, with every edit versioned and attributable like a wiki. The Overpass API and regular data extracts (via providers like Geofabrik) let developers query or download the full dataset for any region.

Because the raw data is open, a broad ecosystem of tools is built on top of it, including custom map styles and tile rendering (OSM Carto and others), routing engines such as OSRM and GraphHopper, and geocoding services, giving developers a full mapping stack without relying on a single proprietary vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenStreetMap's data is free to download and use, including for commercial products, under the ODbL, which requires attribution and that derivative databases remain similarly open.

The Foundation does not charge for map data or the main openstreetmap.org tiles for light usage, but heavy commercial use of the public tile servers is discouraged; instead, most production applications pay third-party providers such as Mapbox, MapTiler, or Geofabrik for hosted tiles, geocoding, and routing built on OSM data, with pricing set independently by each provider.

features.title: Community-editable map data
features.description: Any registered user can add or correct map features using the iD or JOSM editors, with full version history for every change.

features.title: Open Database License (ODbL)
features.description: Map data can be freely used, modified, and redistributed for any purpose, including commercial, as long as attribution is given and derivative databases stay open.

features.title: Overpass API and data extracts
features.description: Developers can query specific feature types in a region via the Overpass API or download full regional extracts from mirrors like Geofabrik.

features.title: Global, granular coverage
features.description: Covers roads, trails, buildings, land use, points of interest, and administrative boundaries down to a very local level, especially strong in areas mapped by active local communities.

features.title: Humanitarian mapping (HOT)
features.description: The Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team coordinates volunteers to rapidly map disaster zones and underserved regions to support relief organizations.

features.title: Third-party tile, routing, and geocoding services
features.description: An ecosystem of providers (Mapbox, MapTiler, OSRM, GraphHopper, Nominatim) build hosted maps, routing, and address search on top of the open OSM dataset.

pros: Map data is free to use commercially with no per-request licensing fees, only attribution.
Extremely detailed and current in areas with active local mapping communities.
Fully transparent and auditable data, with no single company controlling what's shown.
Large open-source ecosystem of routing, geocoding, and rendering tools built on top of it.
Not subject to a single vendor's pricing changes or API shutdowns.
cons: Data quality and completeness vary significantly by region depending on volunteer activity.
No official customer support; help comes from community forums, mailing lists, and wikis.
Attribution and ODbL share-alike requirements can be a compliance burden for some commercial products.
Raw data requires technical work (rendering, hosting, indexing) to turn into a usable map product.
Editing conventions and tagging schemes have a learning curve for new contributors.

faqs.question: Is OpenStreetMap free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. OSM's map data is free to download, use, and modify, including for commercial products, under the Open Database License, which requires attribution.

faqs.question: Who owns OpenStreetMap?
faqs.answer: No single company owns OSM. The data belongs collectively to its contributors, while the OpenStreetMap Foundation, a UK nonprofit, owns and operates the underlying infrastructure and holds the license on the community's behalf.

faqs.question: How is OpenStreetMap different from Google Maps?
faqs.answer: OpenStreetMap's underlying data is open, editable, and free to use commercially with attribution, while Google Maps data is proprietary and access is metered through paid API usage.

faqs.question: Who edits OpenStreetMap?
faqs.answer: Millions of registered volunteers worldwide edit OSM using tools like the iD web editor or the JOSM desktop editor, sourcing changes from GPS traces, imagery, and local surveys.

faqs.question: Can I use OpenStreetMap data in a commercial app?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as you comply with the Open Database License, which primarily requires giving appropriate attribution to OpenStreetMap contributors.

faqs.question: What is the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team?
faqs.answer: HOT is a nonprofit that coordinates volunteer mapping efforts, often rapidly mapping disaster-affected or under-mapped regions to support humanitarian and relief organizations.

faqs.question: Do I need to pay to display OpenStreetMap tiles on my website?
faqs.answer: Light use of the public openstreetmap.org tile servers is free, but heavy commercial traffic is expected to use a paid third-party tile provider such as Mapbox or MapTiler instead.

use_cases.title: Mobile and web app mapping
use_cases.description: Developers embed OSM-based maps, geocoding, and routing into apps without per-request licensing fees, often via a commercial tile provider built on OSM data.

use_cases.title: Disaster response and humanitarian aid
use_cases.description: Volunteer mapping communities rapidly survey and map crisis-affected regions to give relief organizations accurate, up-to-date geographic data.

use_cases.title: GIS research and civic technology
use_cases.description: Researchers, urban planners, and civic-tech projects use OSM's open, inspectable data for analysis and public-facing map tools where data transparency matters.
use_cases.audience: Researchers, urban planners, and civic-tech teams

features: Full access to the global OSM dataset
Free for commercial and non-commercial use with attribution
Downloadable extracts and Overpass API access
Geocoding and routing APIs built on OSM data
Offered independently by providers like Mapbox, MapTiler, and Geofabrik

enrichment.short_description: OpenTable is an online and mobile restaurant reservation and guest-management platform connecting diners with tens of thousands of restaurants worldwide, owned by Booking Holdings.
enrichment.seo_title: OpenTable Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenTable in 2026: restaurant plan pricing, per-cover fees, key GuestCenter features, and how it compares to Resy, Tock, and SevenRooms.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenTable is the best-known online restaurant reservation platform, connecting diners to restaurants for free while charging restaurants a subscription plus per-reservation fees. It was founded on July 2, 1998, in San Francisco by Chuck Templeton, Sid Gorham, and Eric Moe, initially incorporated as easyeats.com before rebranding to OpenTable.com in 1999. The company went public on NASDAQ under the ticker OPEN in 2009, and in 2014 was acquired by The Priceline Group (renamed Booking Holdings in 2018) for approximately $2.6 billion in cash; it now operates as a subsidiary of Booking Holdings alongside Booking.com, Priceline, Kayak, and Agoda. OpenTable connects diners to more than 60,000 restaurants worldwide and facilitates well over a billion diner visits annually through its free consumer app and website, where users can search availability, book instantly, join dining loyalty programs, and earn OpenTable Dining Points redeemable for discounts. On the restaurant side, OpenTable sells three subscription tiers: Basic at $149/month, Core at $299/month plus $1 per network cover, and Pro at $499/month plus $1 per network cover, with an additional 2% service fee introduced in early 2026 on deposits and prepaid dining experiences. Restaurant-facing tools include the GuestCenter reservation and table-management system, a guest CRM with dining history and preferences, a waitlist and walk-in management feature, POS integrations, marketing placement within OpenTable's network, and review collection. OpenTable's target customers range from independent restaurants needing basic online booking to multi-location restaurant groups and hotels needing centralized guest data across venues. Its main competitors are Resy (owned by American Express, which is merging in Tock, also Amex-owned, to reach over 25,000 combined venues), Yelp Guest Manager, SevenRooms (positioned for enterprise restaurant groups and hospitality brands), and Toast Tables. Many restaurants have publicly cited OpenTable's per-cover fees and 2026 service fee as reasons for evaluating alternatives with flatter pricing. OpenTable remains, however, the largest reservation network by sheer restaurant count and diner reach in most English-speaking markets, making network effects its biggest competitive advantage even as newer platforms compete on pricing and CRM depth.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenTable is a two-sided marketplace: a free consumer app and website for diners to find and book restaurant tables, and a paid subscription platform for restaurants to manage reservations, waitlists, and guest relationships.

Founded in 1998 and acquired by Booking Holdings in 2014, OpenTable operates one of the largest restaurant reservation networks in the world, spanning more than 60,000 restaurants across dozens of countries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: For diners, OpenTable offers real-time table availability search, instant online booking, a loyalty points program, and personalized restaurant recommendations. For restaurants, the GuestCenter platform combines a reservation book, table and floor-plan management, a waitlist and walk-in queue, and a guest CRM that tracks dining history, preferences, and special occasions.

Restaurants also get access to marketing placement across OpenTable's network and mobile apps, automated guest review collection, and integrations with point-of-sale and payment systems, which together are designed to both fill tables and deepen repeat-guest relationships.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Diners never pay to search for or book a table on OpenTable; the platform monetizes exclusively on the restaurant side.

Restaurants choose between three monthly plans: Basic ($149/month, flat), Core ($299/month plus $1 per online 'network' cover), and Pro ($499/month plus $1 per network cover, with deeper CRM and marketing tools). As of early 2026, OpenTable also applies a 2% service fee on deposits and prepaid dining experiences booked through the platform.

features.title: GuestCenter reservation and table management
features.description: A restaurant-facing system for managing bookings, table assignments, and floor plans in real time.

features.title: Guest CRM and dining history
features.description: Tracks guest preferences, visit history, spend, and special occasions to help restaurants personalize service and target repeat business.

features.title: Waitlist and walk-in management
features.description: Digitally manages walk-in queues and quoted wait times alongside standard reservations.

features.title: Diner mobile app and loyalty program
features.description: A free consumer app for searching, booking, and reviewing restaurants, with OpenTable Dining Points earned on qualifying reservations.

features.title: Network marketing and discovery
features.description: Restaurant listings surface across OpenTable's site, app, and partner placements to attract new diners.

features.title: POS and payment integrations
features.description: Connects with point-of-sale systems and supports deposits and prepaid experiences for high-demand bookings.

features.title: Review collection
features.description: Automatically prompts diners to leave verified reviews after a completed reservation.

pros: Largest and most widely recognized restaurant reservation network, driving significant diner discovery traffic.
Free for diners with no booking fees on the consumer side.
Mature, full-featured guest CRM and table-management tooling on higher-tier plans.
Backed by Booking Holdings, giving it financial stability and distribution muscle.
Well-established integrations with major POS and payment providers.
cons: Per-cover fees on Core and Pro plans can add up quickly for high-volume restaurants.
2026 service fee on deposits and prepaid experiences adds another cost layer.
Lower-tier Basic plan has limited CRM and marketing functionality.
Restaurants compete for visibility within the same network as nearby competitors.
Newer competitors like Resy and Tock offer more flexible pricing models for some restaurant types.

faqs.question: Is OpenTable free for diners?
faqs.answer: Yes, searching for and booking a table on OpenTable is completely free for diners; OpenTable charges restaurants, not guests, for reservations.

faqs.question: How much does OpenTable cost for restaurants?
faqs.answer: Restaurants choose from three plans: Basic at $149/month, Core at $299/month plus $1 per network cover, and Pro at $499/month plus $1 per network cover; a 2% service fee also applies to deposits and prepaid experiences as of 2026.

faqs.question: Who owns OpenTable?
faqs.answer: OpenTable has been a subsidiary of Booking Holdings (NASDAQ: BKNG) since it was acquired from The Priceline Group in 2014 for about $2.6 billion.

faqs.question: When was OpenTable founded?
faqs.answer: OpenTable was founded on July 2, 1998, in San Francisco by Chuck Templeton, Sid Gorham, and Eric Moe.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to OpenTable?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Resy and Tock (both owned by American Express), Yelp Guest Manager, SevenRooms, and Toast Tables, each with different pricing models and target restaurant segments.

faqs.question: What is OpenTable Dining Points?
faqs.answer: It is OpenTable's diner loyalty program, where guests earn points for completing eligible reservations that can be redeemed for dining credit.

faqs.question: Does OpenTable work outside the United States?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenTable operates internationally in markets including the UK, Germany, Mexico, and Japan, though restaurant density and plan availability vary by market.

use_cases.title: Diner table booking
use_cases.description: Consumers search real-time availability and book tables instantly at nearby or destination restaurants without calling ahead.

use_cases.title: Independent restaurant reservation and waitlist management
use_cases.description: Single-location restaurants use OpenTable's Basic or Core plans to manage online bookings, walk-in queues, and table assignments.

use_cases.title: Multi-location guest relationship management
use_cases.description: Restaurant groups and hospitality brands use Pro-tier GuestCenter CRM to unify guest profiles and marketing across multiple venues.
use_cases.audience: Restaurant groups and hotel F&B operators

enrichment.short_description: OpenTelemetry (OTel) is a vendor-neutral, open-source observability framework providing APIs, SDKs, and a collector to generate, collect, and export traces, metrics, and logs from applications.
enrichment.company_size: CNCF graduated project; 12,000+ contributors from 2,800+ companies (not a single company)
enrichment.headquarters: Governed by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), San Francisco, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: OpenTelemetry Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenTelemetry in 2026: what the CNCF-graduated observability standard covers, supported languages, how it works with backends, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenTelemetry (often shortened to OTel) is a vendor-neutral, open-source framework of APIs, SDKs, and tooling for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data, specifically distributed traces, metrics, and logs, from instrumented applications. It was formed in 2019 through the merger of two earlier, competing observability standards: OpenTracing (focused on distributed tracing) and OpenCensus (originally created at Google, covering tracing and metrics). Rather than let the ecosystem fragment between two overlapping specifications, their maintainers combined efforts under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), entering the CNCF Sandbox in 2019, reaching Incubating status in August 2021, and being announced as a Graduated CNCF project in May 2026, CNCF's highest maturity tier alongside Kubernetes and Prometheus. OpenTelemetry is not a company or a commercial product; it is an open standard and reference implementation maintained by a large, distributed community, reported by CNCF to include more than 12,000 contributors from over 2,800 companies. It provides consistent, auto- and manually-instrumentable SDKs for more than a dozen languages, including Java, Python, Go, JavaScript/Node.js, .NET, Ruby, PHP, C++, Rust, Swift, and Erlang/Elixir, plus the OpenTelemetry Collector, a vendor-agnostic agent/gateway that receives, processes, and exports telemetry data using the OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP). Crucially, OpenTelemetry defines how telemetry is generated and transmitted, but does not provide a storage backend, dashboard, or alerting product itself; it is designed to plug into a wide range of observability backends interchangeably. This makes OpenTelemetry's primary value proposition avoiding vendor lock-in: teams instrument their code once against a neutral standard and can switch or run multiple observability backends, such as Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace, Splunk, Grafana/Prometheus, Honeycomb, or an open-source stack, without re-instrumenting their applications. Its target audience is platform engineering, SRE, and DevOps teams at organizations of any size running microservices or cloud-native architectures who want standardized, portable telemetry across polyglot codebases. Because nearly every major observability vendor now natively supports OTLP ingestion, OpenTelemetry has effectively become the de facto industry standard for instrumentation, and most modern APM and observability tools are better described as OpenTelemetry-compatible backends than as competitors to OpenTelemetry itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenTelemetry is an open-source, vendor-neutral standard for instrumenting applications to produce traces, metrics, and logs. It exists to solve fragmentation: before OTel, teams often had to choose between multiple incompatible tracing and metrics libraries, each tied to a specific observability vendor.

Formed in 2019 from the merger of OpenTracing and OpenCensus, OpenTelemetry is hosted by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) and reached CNCF's Graduated status in May 2026, its highest level of project maturity, reserved for widely adopted, production-proven projects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenTelemetry provides language SDKs and auto-instrumentation agents for more than a dozen popular languages, standardized semantic conventions for naming telemetry data consistently across services, and the OpenTelemetry Collector, a configurable pipeline that can receive, batch, transform, and export telemetry to one or more backends.

Because everything speaks a common protocol (OTLP), teams can capture traces, metrics, and logs once and route them to any compatible backend, switch backends without re-instrumenting code, or fan out the same telemetry to multiple destinations at once, such as a security data lake and an APM tool simultaneously.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenTelemetry itself is entirely free and open source, licensed under Apache 2.0, with no paid tier, seat licenses, or usage limits imposed by the project.

The cost that organizations actually incur comes from the observability backend they choose to send OTel data to, whether that is a commercial APM vendor billed by ingested data volume or hosts, or infrastructure costs for self-hosting an open-source stack like Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Official instrumentation libraries for Java, Python, Go, Node.js, .NET, Ruby, PHP, C++, Rust, Swift, Erlang/Elixir, and more.

features.title: Auto-instrumentation
features.description: Agents that instrument popular frameworks and libraries automatically with minimal code changes, especially mature for Java and .NET.

features.title: OpenTelemetry Collector
features.description: A standalone, vendor-agnostic pipeline for receiving, processing, batching, and exporting telemetry to one or more backends.

features.title: Unified traces, metrics, and logs
features.description: A single instrumentation model covers all three telemetry signal types instead of requiring separate libraries for each.

features.title: OTLP protocol
features.description: A standardized wire protocol supported natively by nearly every major observability backend, avoiding custom export formats.

features.title: Semantic conventions
features.description: Standardized naming and attribute schemas so telemetry from different services and languages stays consistent and comparable.

features.title: Backend portability
features.description: Instrument once, then send data to Datadog, New Relic, Dynatrace, Grafana, Honeycomb, or a self-hosted stack without re-instrumenting.

pros: Completely free, open source, and vendor-neutral, avoiding lock-in to a single observability vendor.
CNCF Graduated status signals production maturity and long-term project stability.
Broad language and framework coverage with strong auto-instrumentation for major stacks.
Now supported natively by nearly every major APM and observability backend.
Backed by a large, active community and contributions from major cloud and observability vendors.
cons: Does not include a storage backend, dashboard, or alerting; you must pair it with a separate observability tool.
Instrumentation quality and auto-instrumentation maturity still vary by language and framework.
Collector configuration and pipeline tuning can be complex for advanced, multi-backend setups.
Semantic conventions have evolved over time, occasionally requiring migration work for existing instrumentation.
Steeper initial learning curve than a single vendor's proprietary, all-in-one instrumentation agent.

faqs.question: Is OpenTelemetry free?
faqs.answer: Yes. OpenTelemetry is fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license with no cost to use. You only pay for whatever backend you choose to store and analyze the telemetry it produces.

faqs.question: Who created OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: OpenTelemetry was formed in 2019 by merging two earlier projects, OpenTracing and OpenCensus, under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF).

faqs.question: Does OpenTelemetry replace tools like Datadog or New Relic?
faqs.answer: No. OpenTelemetry standardizes how telemetry is generated and transmitted; it still needs a backend like Datadog, New Relic, Grafana, or an open-source stack to store, visualize, and alert on the data.

faqs.question: What is the OpenTelemetry Collector?
faqs.answer: It is a vendor-agnostic service that receives telemetry from your applications, processes or batches it, and exports it to one or more observability backends, decoupling instrumentation from any specific vendor.

faqs.question: What languages does OpenTelemetry support?
faqs.answer: OpenTelemetry provides official SDKs for more than a dozen languages, including Java, Python, Go, JavaScript/Node.js, .NET, Ruby, PHP, C++, Rust, and Erlang/Elixir.

faqs.question: Is OpenTelemetry production-ready?
faqs.answer: Yes. CNCF announced OpenTelemetry's graduation, its highest maturity status, in May 2026, reflecting widespread production adoption across the industry.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OpenTracing, OpenCensus, and OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: OpenTracing and OpenCensus were two earlier, competing telemetry standards; they merged in 2019 to form OpenTelemetry, which is now the actively maintained successor to both.

use_cases.title: Microservices instrumentation
use_cases.description: Engineering teams instrument distributed, polyglot microservices once with OpenTelemetry to get consistent traces and metrics across every service.

use_cases.title: Vendor-neutral observability strategy
use_cases.description: Organizations standardize on OTel to avoid rewriting instrumentation if they switch or evaluate observability backends over time.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises evaluating or migrating observability vendors

use_cases.title: Multi-backend telemetry routing
use_cases.description: Teams use the OpenTelemetry Collector to fan the same telemetry out to multiple destinations, such as a cost-effective long-term store and a real-time alerting tool.

features: Full access to all SDKs, the Collector, and specifications
No usage limits or paid tiers

enrichment.short_description: OpenTimeClock (Open Time Clock) is a web-based employee time clock and workforce management SaaS that lets businesses track attendance, hours, PTO, and payroll-ready timesheets, with a free tier for unlimited users.
enrichment.headquarters: Not publicly disclosed; customer support operates on U.S. Eastern Time
enrichment.seo_title: OpenTimeClock Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenTimeClock in 2026: free and paid plan pricing, GPS and facial-recognition clock-in, PTO tracking, and how it compares to other time clock software.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenTimeClock, also styled Open Time Clock or abbreviated OTC, is a web-based employee time and attendance tracking service aimed at small and mid-sized businesses, nonprofits, and organizations that need an affordable alternative to enterprise workforce-management suites. The company states it has been in business since 1997, and its data is hosted on Amazon Web Services in the US East (N. Virginia) region; the company does not publicly disclose a physical headquarters address, though customer support is offered by phone during Eastern Time business hours. OpenTimeClock's core differentiator is its free tier: unlike most time-tracking competitors, the Free Plan supports an unlimited number of employees and unlimited departments at no cost, with the main limitation being that PDF/Excel report exports are not included after an initial 30-day trial period (CSV export and on-screen reporting remain available). Businesses that need ongoing PDF/Excel exports and premium features upgrade to the Paid Plan, priced at $39/month or $399/year (billed annually, equivalent to about $33.25/month) for unlimited users, which unlocks features such as shift scheduling, PTO/vacation accrual tracking, manager approval workflows, job/project cost tracking, GPS-based clock-in verification, and payroll-ready export formats. OpenTimeClock also offers an unusual 'Sponsor Link' plan, letting organizations unlock Paid Plan features for free in exchange for placing a text link back to Open Time Clock on their website's homepage, and typically extends discounted or free access to qualifying nonprofit organizations (NPOs). Clock-in methods supported include web browser, mobile app, GPS geofencing, and facial recognition, along with offline clock-in capability for locations with unreliable connectivity. The product is aimed squarely at cost-sensitive small businesses, franchises, schools, construction/field-service companies, and nonprofits that need reliable time and attendance tracking without the per-user pricing common among competitors like When I Work, Homebase, TSheets/QuickBooks Time, or Deputy. Because unlimited users are included even on the free tier, OpenTimeClock is frequently chosen by organizations with large or fluctuating headcounts, such as schools or seasonal employers, where per-seat competitor pricing would be cost-prohibitive.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenTimeClock is a web-based time and attendance system built for small and mid-sized organizations that need to track employee hours, breaks, and paid time off without paying per-user fees. It has operated since 1997 and runs on Amazon Web Services infrastructure.

Its standout feature is an unlimited-user free tier, a rarity in the time-tracking category where most competitors charge per employee per month. Paid upgrades add reporting exports and advanced scheduling, approval, and job-costing features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Employees can clock in and out from a web browser, mobile app, or a shared kiosk device, with optional GPS geofencing and facial recognition to verify identity and location at clock-in. Managers get timesheet approval workflows, PTO and vacation accrual tracking, shift scheduling, and job or project cost tracking for billing purposes.

Reporting tools support payroll processing with exportable timesheets, and the system offers offline clock-in support for locations with unreliable internet, syncing entries once connectivity returns.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Free Plan includes unlimited employees and departments; its main restriction is that PDF/Excel report exports require a paid upgrade after an initial 30-day trial window, though CSV and on-screen reporting remain available indefinitely.

The Paid Plan costs $39/month, or $399/year when billed annually (about $33.25/month), also for unlimited users, and unlocks report exports, shift scheduling, PTO accrual, GPS tracking, and job costing. A Sponsor Link option lets organizations get Paid Plan features free in exchange for a homepage text-link back to Open Time Clock, and discounted access is generally available to qualifying nonprofits.

features.title: Unlimited-user free tier
features.description: The Free Plan supports an unlimited number of employees and departments at no cost, unusual for the time-tracking category.

features.title: GPS and geofenced clock-in
features.description: Verifies employee location at clock-in and clock-out to help prevent time theft and buddy punching for field or multi-site teams.

features.title: Facial recognition clock-in
features.description: Optional biometric verification adds an extra layer of identity confirmation at the time clock.

features.title: PTO and accrual tracking
features.description: Tracks vacation, sick leave, and other paid-time-off accruals and balances automatically.

features.title: Shift scheduling
features.description: Lets managers build and publish employee shift schedules alongside time tracking.

features.title: Job and project cost tracking
features.description: Allows employees to log hours against specific jobs, clients, or projects for cost and billing analysis.

features.title: Offline clock-in support
features.description: Employees can clock in without an internet connection, with entries syncing automatically once connectivity is restored.

pros: Free plan supports unlimited employees, which is rare among time-tracking competitors that charge per seat.
Paid plan is inexpensive relative to per-user competitors, especially for larger teams.
Supports multiple clock-in methods, including GPS, facial recognition, and offline mode.
Includes job costing and PTO accrual tracking typically reserved for pricier platforms.
Nonprofit-friendly pricing and a free 'Sponsor Link' option for cost-conscious organizations.
cons: Free plan loses PDF/Excel report export after the initial 30-day trial period.
Company does not publicly disclose a physical headquarters or leadership team, which may concern due-diligence-conscious buyers.
Interface and design feel dated compared to newer, VC-backed time-tracking competitors.
Smaller integration ecosystem with payroll and HR platforms than larger competitors like TSheets/QuickBooks Time or Deputy.
Customer support is phone/email based with limited hours rather than 24/7 live chat.

faqs.question: Is OpenTimeClock really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free Plan supports unlimited employees at no cost. The main limitation is that PDF/Excel report exports are only free for the first 30 days, after which they require the Paid Plan.

faqs.question: How much does the OpenTimeClock Paid Plan cost?
faqs.answer: The Paid Plan is $39/month billed monthly, or $399/year billed annually (about $33.25/month), and covers unlimited users.

faqs.question: Does OpenTimeClock support GPS tracking?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports GPS-based clock-in verification and geofencing so managers can confirm employees clocked in from an approved location.

faqs.question: Can nonprofits get a discount on OpenTimeClock?
faqs.answer: OpenTimeClock offers reduced or free access to qualifying nonprofit organizations (NPOs) under its NPO program.

faqs.question: What is the Sponsor Link plan?
faqs.answer: It lets an organization unlock Paid Plan features for free by placing a text link to Open Time Clock or 'Time Clock Software' on their website's homepage.

faqs.question: Does OpenTimeClock work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, employees can clock in without an internet connection and entries will sync automatically once the device reconnects.

faqs.question: How long has OpenTimeClock been in business?
faqs.answer: The company states it has been in business since 1997, though its current web-based product line has evolved considerably since then.

use_cases.title: Small business payroll-ready time tracking
use_cases.description: Small businesses use the free or paid plan to track hourly employee attendance and export data for payroll processing.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and office managers

use_cases.title: Multi-site and field-service workforce tracking
use_cases.description: Companies with mobile or multi-location employees use GPS geofencing and offline clock-in to verify attendance away from a central office.
use_cases.audience: Construction, field service, and multi-location businesses

use_cases.title: Nonprofit and school attendance tracking
use_cases.description: Nonprofits and schools with large or fluctuating headcounts use the unlimited-user free tier to track staff or volunteer hours without per-seat costs.

features: PDF/Excel export free for first 30 days
Same features as monthly Paid Plan

pricing_plans.price: Free (in exchange for a homepage link)
features: Requires a text link to Open Time Clock on your homepage

enrichment.short_description: OpenTripPlanner (OTP) is an open-source, multimodal trip-planning engine that computes public transit, walking, cycling, and driving itineraries, used by transit agencies and mobility developers worldwide.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source project maintained by a global community of transit agencies and contributors, not a single company
enrichment.headquarters: Originated at TriMet in Portland, Oregon, United States; now governed by an international Project Leadership Committee
enrichment.seo_title: OpenTripPlanner Review 2026: Features, Deployment and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenTripPlanner in 2026: how the open-source multimodal trip-planning engine works, OTP2 routing, GTFS and NeTEx support, and deployment costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenTripPlanner (OTP) is a free, open-source, multimodal trip-planning engine that calculates itineraries combining public transit, walking, cycling, and driving. It originated in July 2009 when Portland, Oregon's transit agency, TriMet, used a Regional Travel Options grant to host a three-day kickoff workshop bringing together transit agencies and the developers of the era's leading open-source transit routing tools, David Emory of FivePoints, Brian Ferris of OneBusAway, and Brandon Martin-Anderson of Graphserver, to build a unified successor. From 2009 through 2012, development was coordinated by the New York nonprofit OpenPlans, after which stewardship shifted to a distributed, international community of contributing transit agencies, consultancies, and individual developers organized around a Project Leadership Committee; no single company owns OTP, and governance requires changes to be reviewed and approved by contributors from at least two different organizations. OTP ingests standard transit data formats, primarily GTFS (General Transit Feed Specification) for schedules and, since the OTP2 rewrite, the EU-standard NeTEx format, plus OpenStreetMap data for the street, path, and cycling network, to compute itineraries. A major architectural overhaul, OTP2, was initiated in 2018 when the Norwegian transit organizations Ruter and Entur led the integration of a new round-based transit routing engine inspired by the R5 routing algorithm; OTP2 entered feature freeze in September 2020 and was released at the end of November 2020, delivering major performance gains and full NeTEx compatibility for European regulatory compliance. OpenTripPlanner is deployed by transit agencies and cities worldwide, from TriMet's own regional trip planner in Portland to national-level deployments in Norway (Entur), and is embedded in numerous public-facing trip-planning apps and mobility-as-a-service (MaaS) platforms. Its target audience is public transit agencies, municipal and regional transportation authorities, and mobility developers who need a self-hosted, customizable routing engine rather than a proprietary API, often for reasons of cost control, data sovereignty, or the ability to model agency-specific fare and accessibility rules. Because it is open source under the LGPL, there is no license fee; costs come from hosting, data preparation, and any commercial support or integration work purchased from firms such as Conveyal, Entur, or Cambridge Systematics. Its closest alternatives include Google's proprietary transit directions API, Conveyal's R5 engine (which influenced OTP2's routing core), Motion Tag, and Google's open GTFS ecosystem tools more broadly, though OTP remains one of the only fully open-source, self-hostable multimodal routing engines with wide agency adoption.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenTripPlanner is an open-source routing engine that computes multimodal trip itineraries, combining public transit schedules with walking, cycling, and driving networks to answer 'how do I get from A to B' queries.

It began in 2009 as a TriMet-funded effort to unify several competing open-source transit routing tools into a single successor project, and has since grown into a globally used platform maintained by an international community of transit agencies and developers rather than any single company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OTP ingests GTFS transit schedule data and OpenStreetMap street/path data to build a routable graph, then computes itineraries across transit, walking, biking, and driving, including real-time updates via GTFS-Realtime feeds for delays and service changes. The OTP2 release introduced a round-based transit routing engine inspired by R5 for much faster performance, along with support for the EU's NeTEx data standard.

The engine exposes REST and GraphQL APIs for integration into web and mobile trip-planning applications, supports accessibility-aware routing such as wheelchair-accessible itineraries, and can calculate fares for supported transit systems, all while being fully self-hostable on an agency's own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenTripPlanner is free and open source under the LGPL license, so there is no software cost to download, deploy, or modify it.

Real-world costs come from server hosting, preparing and maintaining GTFS/NeTEx and OSM data feeds, and any optional commercial integration, hosting, or support services purchased from firms in the OTP ecosystem, which are typically quoted on a custom basis.

features.title: Multimodal itinerary planning
features.description: Computes trip options combining public transit, walking, cycling, and driving in a single routing engine.

features.title: GTFS and GTFS-Realtime support
features.description: Ingests standard transit schedule data and applies real-time delay and service-change updates.

features.title: NeTEx data support
features.description: Supports the EU-standard NeTEx transit data format, enabling regulatory compliance for European deployments.

features.title: OTP2 round-based routing engine
features.description: A high-performance transit routing core, inspired by the R5 algorithm, introduced in the 2020 OTP2 release for faster, more scalable queries.

features.title: REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Exposes standard APIs for integrating trip-planning results into web and mobile applications.

features.title: Accessibility-aware routing
features.description: Supports wheelchair-accessible itinerary options for riders with mobility needs.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs entirely on an agency's own infrastructure, giving full control over data and uptime rather than relying on a third-party API.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing fees, ideal for public agencies with limited budgets.
Proven at scale across many transit agencies and national deployments worldwide.
Supports both GTFS and the EU's NeTEx standard, useful for both American and European agencies.
OTP2's routing engine delivers strong performance even for large, complex transit networks.
Governed by an open, multi-organization process rather than a single vendor's roadmap.
cons: Requires in-house or contracted engineering expertise to deploy, configure, and maintain.
No official vendor support; help comes from community channels and paid consultancies.
Data preparation (clean GTFS/OSM feeds) is a significant, ongoing operational burden.
Less polished out-of-the-box UI compared to consumer-facing proprietary map/transit apps.
Smaller ecosystem of turnkey hosting options compared to commercial routing APIs.

faqs.question: Is OpenTripPlanner free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenTripPlanner is open source under the LGPL license and free to download, deploy, and modify. Costs come from hosting, data preparation, and any optional paid support.

faqs.question: Who created OpenTripPlanner?
faqs.answer: It originated in 2009 through a project funded by TriMet, Portland's transit agency, which brought together the developers of several earlier open-source transit routing tools to build a unified successor.

faqs.question: What data does OpenTripPlanner use?
faqs.answer: It uses GTFS (and GTFS-Realtime for live updates) or NeTEx for transit schedules, combined with OpenStreetMap data for streets, paths, and cycling infrastructure.

faqs.question: What is OTP2?
faqs.answer: OTP2 is a major 2020 rewrite of OpenTripPlanner's transit routing engine, inspired by the R5 algorithm, that significantly improved performance and added support for the EU's NeTEx data standard.

faqs.question: Who uses OpenTripPlanner?
faqs.answer: Public transit agencies, regional transportation authorities, and mobility developers use OTP, including TriMet in Portland and national deployments such as Entur in Norway.

faqs.question: Can I get commercial support for OpenTripPlanner?
faqs.answer: Yes, several firms including Conveyal and Cambridge Systematics offer paid hosting, integration, and support services built around OTP, typically quoted per project.

faqs.question: Does OpenTripPlanner support real-time transit updates?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can ingest GTFS-Realtime feeds to factor in live delays, cancellations, and service changes when computing itineraries.

use_cases.title: Transit agency trip planners
use_cases.description: Transit agencies deploy OTP to power the official rider-facing trip planner on their website or app, covering their own service area.

use_cases.title: Mobility-as-a-service (MaaS) platforms
use_cases.description: Cities and mobility startups integrate OTP's multimodal routing to combine transit with bikeshare, scooters, and other modes in one planning experience.
use_cases.audience: MaaS platforms and city mobility programs

use_cases.title: Transportation research and analysis
use_cases.description: Researchers and consultancies use OTP's routing engine to model accessibility, travel times, and network scenarios for planning studies.

features: Available from firms such as Conveyal and Cambridge Systematics

enrichment.short_description: OpenTTD is a free, open-source business simulation game that reimplements and expands the 1995 classic Transport Tycoon Deluxe, tasking players with building rail, road, air, and sea transport networks.
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer open-source community project; no company
enrichment.headquarters: Originated in Sweden (creator Ludvig Strigeus); community-developed and distributed via openttd.org
enrichment.seo_title: OpenTTD Review 2026: Features, Gameplay and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenTTD in 2026: how the free, open-source Transport Tycoon Deluxe remake plays, its modding ecosystem, multiplayer, and alternatives for tycoon fans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenTTD is a free, open-source business simulation video game that reverse-engineers and substantially expands Chris Sawyer's 1995 classic Transport Tycoon Deluxe. Swedish programmer Ludvig Strigeus began reverse-engineering the original game and converting it to C in 2003, contacting Owen Rudge, owner of the TT-Forums community, to share his plans; the first working release was published on March 6, 2004, under the name OpenTTD. What started as one developer's personal project quickly grew into a community-driven effort, and OpenTTD is now maintained by a large, ongoing volunteer team under the GNU General Public License version 2. In the original game, players build and operate transport networks, trains, road vehicles, ships, and aircraft, connecting cities and industries to move passengers and cargo for profit across a persistent, growing simulated economy and map. OpenTTD preserves this core gameplay loop while adding a long list of features the original never had: much larger and more varied procedurally generated maps, support for dozens of languages, custom scriptable AI opponents and game scripts, extensive mod support via the NewGRF format (custom vehicles, graphics, and scenarios), robust multiplayer over local networks or the internet, and native ports for Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD. Because the original game's proprietary graphics and sound assets could not legally be redistributed, the community also created free replacement asset packs, OpenGFX, OpenSFX, and OpenMSX, so OpenTTD can be played entirely without owning the original commercial game. OpenTTD received recognition early on, including the most votes for Game of the Year in the 2004 Amiga Games Award, and has remained in continuous active development for over two decades, a rarity among fan remakes. It targets hobbyist strategy and simulation gamers, especially fans of classic tycoon and transport-management games, as well as modders and scripters who enjoy building custom AI opponents, scenarios, or graphics packs. Its closest peers are other classic-game remakes and successors such as Locomotion, Transport Fever, Cities: Skylines' transport-focused mods, and Simutrans, though OpenTTD is distinguished by being completely free, fully open source, cross-platform, and still actively updated by its original community more than 20 years after launch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenTTD is a free, open-source recreation and expansion of Transport Tycoon Deluxe, the 1995 business simulation game where players design and operate transport networks to move passengers and cargo for profit. It has been developed continuously by a volunteer community since its first release in 2004.

Built by reverse-engineering the original game and rewriting it in C, OpenTTD preserves the addictive core loop of the original while modernizing it with larger maps, scripting, mod support, and cross-platform availability.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Players construct rail lines, roads, shipping routes, and airports to connect towns and industries, managing vehicle fleets, schedules, and finances across a simulated, growing economy. OpenTTD supports custom AI opponents and game scripts, letting players automate or challenge themselves against scripted competitors.

An extensive NewGRF modding ecosystem lets the community add new vehicles, graphics sets, scenarios, and gameplay mechanics, while free asset packs (OpenGFX, OpenSFX, OpenMSX) mean the game can be played fully without owning the original commercial release. It also supports online and LAN multiplayer and runs natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OpenTTD is completely free to download and play, with no purchase price, subscription, or in-game monetization of any kind.

The project is funded entirely through voluntary community contributions of time and, occasionally, donations to support server and development infrastructure, rather than any commercial revenue model.

features.title: Transport network building
features.description: Design and operate rail, road, sea, and air transport routes connecting towns and industries across a large simulated map.

features.title: NewGRF mod support
features.description: A rich modding format that lets the community add custom vehicles, graphics, scenarios, and gameplay mechanics.

features.title: Free replacement asset packs
features.description: OpenGFX, OpenSFX, and OpenMSX provide free graphics, sound, and music, so the game can be played without the original commercial title.

features.title: Custom AI and game scripts
features.description: Players can script AI opponents or automate parts of their own gameplay using OpenTTD's scripting API.

features.title: Multiplayer support
features.description: Play cooperatively or competitively with others over LAN or the internet.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Native ports for Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD keep the game accessible across operating systems.

features.title: Large, customizable maps
features.description: Procedurally generated maps far larger and more varied than the original game's, with extensive scenario editing tools.

pros: Completely free with no purchase price or in-game monetization.
Actively developed and updated for over two decades by a dedicated volunteer community.
Deep modding ecosystem (NewGRF) that keeps adding new content years after release.
Runs natively on Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD.
Can be played entirely with free assets, without needing the original commercial game.
cons: Dated core gameplay loop and UI conventions inherited from a 1995 game may feel unfamiliar to modern strategy gamers.
Steep learning curve for new players unfamiliar with classic tycoon-style transport games.
No official marketing or storefront presence, so discoverability relies on word of mouth and community channels.
Setting up internet multiplayer or custom mod packs can require technical know-how.
No official support channel; help comes from community forums and wikis rather than paid support.

faqs.question: Is OpenTTD free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenTTD is completely free to download and play, with no purchase price or in-game purchases.

faqs.question: Do I need to own the original Transport Tycoon Deluxe to play OpenTTD?
faqs.answer: No. Free community-made asset packs (OpenGFX, OpenSFX, OpenMSX) provide graphics, sound, and music, so OpenTTD can be played fully without owning the original commercial game.

faqs.question: Who created OpenTTD?
faqs.answer: Swedish programmer Ludvig Strigeus began reverse-engineering Transport Tycoon Deluxe in 2003 and released the first version of OpenTTD on March 6, 2004; it has since been maintained by a volunteer open-source community.

faqs.question: What license is OpenTTD released under?
faqs.answer: OpenTTD is released under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), an open-source license.

faqs.question: Does OpenTTD support multiplayer?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenTTD supports both LAN and internet multiplayer, letting players build and compete on shared transport networks.

faqs.question: Can I mod OpenTTD?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenTTD has an extensive modding ecosystem based on the NewGRF format, letting the community add custom vehicles, graphics, and scenarios, plus a scripting API for custom AI opponents.

faqs.question: What platforms does OpenTTD run on?
faqs.answer: OpenTTD has native ports for Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD.

use_cases.title: Classic tycoon and simulation gaming
use_cases.description: Fans of business simulation and transport-management games play OpenTTD for its deep economic and logistics gameplay.

use_cases.title: Retro gaming and preservation
use_cases.description: Players revisit or discover the classic Transport Tycoon Deluxe experience through a modernized, actively maintained free remake.

use_cases.title: Modding and scripting hobby projects
use_cases.description: Hobbyist developers build custom NewGRF content, scenarios, or AI scripts, contributing to OpenTTD's long-running community ecosystem.

features: Full game with no purchase price
Access to community mods and asset packs
Cross-platform on Windows, macOS, Linux, and BSD

enrichment.short_description: OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment Scanner) is a free, open-source network vulnerability scanner that also forms the core scan engine behind Greenbone's commercial vulnerability management products.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); Greenbone Enterprise appliances at custom pricing
enrichment.company_size: Greenbone AG: approximately 100-150 employees (small-to-medium cybersecurity company)
enrichment.seo_title: OpenVAS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenVAS in 2026: how the open-source vulnerability scanner works, its ties to Greenbone, feed pricing tiers, and how it compares to Nessus and Qualys.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment Scanner) is a free, open-source network vulnerability scanner used to identify security weaknesses, misconfigurations, and missing patches across IT infrastructure. It originated as a fork of the Nessus scanning engine after Nessus's developer, Tenable Network Security, changed Nessus to a proprietary license in October 2005, prompting the open-source community to continue development independently under the OpenVAS name. Shortly afterward, in 2008, the German company Greenbone was founded in Osnabruck, Germany, specifically to provide professional development, support, and commercial products built around OpenVAS; Greenbone remains headquartered in Osnabruck today and has grown into a cybersecurity vendor of roughly 100 to 150 employees serving more than 100,000 installations across over 150 countries. Since 2006, OpenVAS's development has been driven primarily by Greenbone, and in 2009 the company began layering additional modules on top of the core scanner to build a full vulnerability management solution. In 2017, Greenbone rebranded the overall product family as Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM), with OpenVAS continuing on as the name of the free, open-source scanning engine at its core, while GVM refers to the broader open-source and commercial framework (scan management, reporting, and a web UI, the Greenbone Security Assistant) built around it. OpenVAS itself remains free and open source, testing targets against a continuously updated feed of vulnerability tests (VTs) covering thousands of CVEs, and supporting both authenticated (credentialed) and unauthenticated network scans. Greenbone monetizes through commercial offerings layered on top of the free core: OPENVAS BASIC, an entry-level licensed product aimed at small businesses (priced roughly 50 percent below comparable competitor licenses according to Greenbone), and Greenbone Enterprise appliances and enterprise feed subscriptions for larger organizations, both quoted on a custom basis rather than published at a fixed price. OpenVAS's target audience spans security teams, penetration testers, managed security service providers (MSSPs), and compliance-driven organizations that need CVE/CVSS-based vulnerability scanning without the licensing costs of proprietary scanners. Its main competitors are Tenable Nessus (the tool it originally forked from), Qualys VMDR, Rapid7 InsightVM, and Tenable.io, with OpenVAS/Greenbone's key differentiator being its open-source core, GDPR-conscious German origin, and lower-cost commercial tiers relative to those established proprietary vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenVAS is a free, open-source vulnerability scanner used to find security weaknesses, missing patches, and misconfigurations across networked systems. It began as a fork of the Nessus engine after Nessus went proprietary in 2005, and has been developed since 2006 primarily by the German company Greenbone.

Greenbone, founded in Osnabruck, Germany, in 2008, layers commercial products, appliances, and support on top of the free OpenVAS core, branding the overall framework Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM) since 2017, while OpenVAS remains the name of the open-source scan engine at its heart.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OpenVAS scans network targets against a continuously updated feed of vulnerability tests covering thousands of known CVEs, supporting both unauthenticated network scans and authenticated (credentialed) scans that check for missing patches and misconfigurations at the OS and application level. Results are reported with CVE references and CVSS severity scoring to help teams prioritize remediation.

The broader Greenbone Vulnerability Management framework adds a web-based interface (Greenbone Security Assistant), scan scheduling and reporting, compliance-oriented scan policies, and REST/OSP APIs for integrating scan results into SIEM, ticketing, and other security tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core OpenVAS scanner and the Greenbone Community Edition are free and open source, giving anyone access to vulnerability scanning without a license fee, though the community feed of vulnerability tests updates on a slight delay compared to paid feeds.

Greenbone also sells commercial tiers on top of the open-source core: OPENVAS BASIC is an entry-level licensed product for small businesses priced below comparable competitor tools, while Greenbone Enterprise appliances and enterprise feed subscriptions for larger organizations are quoted individually; exact pricing for both is only available by contacting Greenbone sales.

features.title: Network vulnerability scanning
features.description: Scans hosts and networks for known vulnerabilities, missing patches, and misconfigurations using a continuously updated test feed.

features.title: Authenticated and unauthenticated scans
features.description: Supports both credential-based scans for deeper OS/application-level checks and unauthenticated scans that mimic an external attacker's view.

features.title: CVE and CVSS-based reporting
features.description: Findings are mapped to CVE identifiers and scored with CVSS severity to help teams prioritize remediation.

features.title: Continuously updated vulnerability test (VT) feed
features.description: A regularly refreshed feed of vulnerability tests keeps scan coverage current against newly disclosed CVEs.

features.title: Greenbone Security Assistant web UI
features.description: A web-based dashboard for configuring scans, scheduling, and reviewing results without using the command line.

features.title: Compliance scanning policies
features.description: Pre-built scan configurations aligned with common compliance and hardening benchmarks.

features.title: REST/OSP API integration
features.description: Allows scan results to be pulled into SIEM platforms, ticketing systems, and other security workflows.

pros: Core scanner is free and fully open source, with no license fee for the community edition.
Backed by Greenbone, a dedicated company actively maintaining and extending the project since 2006.
Broad vulnerability test coverage mapped to CVE and CVSS for standardized prioritization.
German, GDPR-conscious origin appeals to privacy- and compliance-sensitive European organizations.
Commercial Greenbone tiers are priced below several established proprietary competitors.
cons: Community feed updates slightly later than Greenbone's paid enterprise feed.
Enterprise and Basic tier pricing is not published; buyers must contact sales for a quote.
Setup and tuning can be more involved than some polished commercial-only scanners.
Smaller company size than large competitors like Tenable or Qualys can mean a smaller partner/integration ecosystem.
Scan performance and false-positive rates require ongoing tuning compared to some mature commercial alternatives.

faqs.question: Is OpenVAS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core OpenVAS scanner and the Greenbone Community Edition are free and open source. Greenbone also sells paid Basic and Enterprise tiers with additional features and faster feed updates.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OpenVAS and Greenbone?
faqs.answer: OpenVAS is the open-source vulnerability scanning engine; Greenbone is the German company that develops it and sells commercial products, appliances, and support built around it, branded as Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM).

faqs.question: Is OpenVAS a fork of Nessus?
faqs.answer: Yes, OpenVAS originated as a fork of the Nessus scanner after Tenable Network Security made Nessus proprietary in October 2005.

faqs.question: Where is Greenbone headquartered?
faqs.answer: Greenbone AG is headquartered in Osnabruck, Germany, where it was founded in 2008.

faqs.question: How does OpenVAS compare to Tenable Nessus?
faqs.answer: Both scan for vulnerabilities using CVE/CVSS-based reporting, but OpenVAS's core is open source and free, while Nessus is a proprietary, commercially licensed product; Greenbone's paid tiers compete directly with Nessus's commercial pricing.

faqs.question: Does OpenVAS support compliance scanning?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes pre-built scan policies aligned with common compliance and security hardening benchmarks in addition to general vulnerability scanning.

faqs.question: Who should use OpenVAS?
faqs.answer: Security teams, penetration testers, and managed security service providers who need CVE/CVSS-based network vulnerability scanning without the licensing cost of a fully proprietary scanner.

use_cases.title: Internal network vulnerability assessment
use_cases.description: Security teams run regular OpenVAS scans across internal networks to find missing patches and misconfigurations before attackers do.

use_cases.title: Managed security services
use_cases.description: MSSPs use OpenVAS or Greenbone's commercial tiers to deliver vulnerability scanning as a service across multiple client environments.

use_cases.title: Compliance and audit scanning
use_cases.description: Organizations use OpenVAS's compliance-oriented scan policies to support regulatory audits and security certification requirements.

features: Priced below comparable competitor licenses per Greenbone
